abstract:
After providing a concise overview of the 1920 division of Cieszyn Silesia—a region historically defined by its multi-ethnic and multilingual character—this article examines the institutional landscape of the Polish minority in the Zaolzie region and the impact of Czech language policy. The core of the study investigates the nuances of contemporary linguistic communication in public spaces. By analyzing both written and spoken discourse across official and unofficial registers, the author explores the distinct functions of the Polish language on the Czech side of the Olza River and the role of the Czech language within the movement of the now-divided city. 

Drawing on authentic linguistic data, the analysis evaluates lexical borrowings from non-Slavic languages, the morphological adaptation of loanwords, the pragmatic use of diminutives, and the challenges posed by interlingual homonymy. Furthermore, the study considers orthographic and graphical elements, such as letterforms and spelling conventions, within their broader cultural contexts. Ultimately, the article demonstrates how bilingualism manifests in the visual and social landscape of these twin border cities, highlighting the asymmetrical ways linguistic identity is expressed on the Polish and Czech sides of the frontier.
